speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you when the Germans invaded Poland? What happened to you in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we was looking for the job because if somebody-- young fellow special-- has not job and capture on the street, what was very popular, because each day 10, 20 times daily you cross the team gendarmes and asking you, come on here. Show me the paper, what you're doing, et cetera. You know, that was the danger. And because in high school, I learned German, because popular in Poland was-- you must know other language. That's first. And popular was German or French. French because the diplomatic at this time was the language. And German because it's our neighbor. Nobody like learn Russian, because Russian was something what nobody like. And I learn German. And before you have the diploma, high school diploma, you must speak good other language. Otherwise, you not pass the exam. That's-- I know German. And during this time, I come back from-- to Warsaw. I was looking for some job where German language is required. And I found something that was in Warsaw-- company from Berlin who-- I think so, before the war the-- the company was a transport company like, you know, a company who pack everything and send in the boxes or something like this. And fort this, was required German language. I took this job, and that was half physical, half, oh, paperwork. And I work there and happened very good for me because this company, after couple of months, took some stolen-- not took, stolen-- some collect and send all arts from museum, from very famous Polish family, what was catalog. That was very easy to find where the arts are, special the people who own a very famous, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not farms, but something better, bigger like farms-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Poland, And like, for example, a [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know the English, [NON-ENGLISH]-- count or something like this. And this company was select to pack this stuff and send to Germany. And later, their underground organization know I am working there and give me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not permission. You must do this. Give the all stuff what was sent-- copy to the underground organization. And they did this. And some very important stuff was sent from near to Warsaw, maybe 10 kilometers from the Warsaw, from Wilanow. That's years, years ago some resident of Polish king who destroyed the court from Tatars. Sobieski from [NON-ENGLISH]. From this castle, was sent many boxes to Germany. And I was working the rent and give the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that any of the artifacts and artwork came from Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sure-- later there, later there. This what was a couple family very rich, who was in Warsaw and collect this stuff. And to us, this house, this building was destroyed. And, really, we not involved in this. Sure, every-- everything was take from the Jewish people, later there-- next couple of years. First, was generally, this what was in museum. What was, for example, in very famous families for thousand years, almost. That was the first. Later, the next couple of years, was done the same for Jewish. Not first year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought all these treasures to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treasure-- that was-- was this organization from Belden was sent Dr. Milleman. I remember the name. Dr. Milleman, this was doctor-- a-- a-- arts specialist. And this doctor go with the-- our company representative. And also, two or three guards-- mean SS guards-- and go with the-- by car, by Mercedes, [LAUGHS] to all places and put everything-- this, you take this. You take this. You take-- You know? It was mentioned to representative of our company what's supposed to be from this place taken out. And sometimes it was big-- many things to pick up from this-- not castle. I have not name from the-- the land owner. That was send some special unit from us, couple of guys, and to me, collect this. I never go out of the Warsaw-- collect this. Because other guys go there. I stayed mostly in Warsaw. We packed all diplomats, for example, because 1939, the German has diplomat, Polish-- foreign diplomats in Berlin. And Poland has in Warsaw. After Poland lose the war, the German-- all diplomats sent to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this stuff, what the people has, we pack. Our company packed and sent to Berlin or to [NON-ENGLISH] Estonia, or [NON-ENGLISH] or French, or something like this. That's was the-- your question was who bring us? Sometimes, was bring by truck and to be packed and to put in labels and sent. Sometime, if it's big stuff, our company send some people there. What was the name of the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my goodness, there's a very popular company in West Europe. Only I really forgot, you know, the-- I tried before give you the name. And typical German name, and still is the same name. This is international. Only part was the-- I think so main office was Berlin, and go all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the things that you packed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands and thousands of different pictures, very valuable pictures. For example, lamp, chandelier, or chair, or table, or something. Everything was sent there. And I don't know where was the local store, because was thousand, thousand pieces. In our magazine in Warsaw, our company, magazine, was warehouse tremendous. Like 20, 50 times bigger than this house. And 20-foot ceiling for example. And from there was sent this up to the direction of the Dr. Milleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our company, office was in the-- main office was in like here is in by itself building. In Warsaw, was one building there and it was Platz Napoleona was working maybe 15 people there. And everybody speak two languages. That's officer company. In the field-- I was working in the field-- you must know German. You know? That's-- And guys like me, was not many because other labor not need to speak German. And all the time some-- and it's most young people-- speak German and translates to the Polish worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you have to speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there everything was-- like here, we must speaking English. Because, otherwise, you cannot communicate it otherwise. All paper supposed to be filled in German. That was the reason. And also, sometime, the owner of these goods, if are diplomats-- mostly in Europe, diplomats this time speak French first, and later learn German. Sometime, speak Polish little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about doing that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, that was not a reason. If you not-- like or not like, that was not the question. First question, you'll be sent to labor camp, or you'll be in a work zone. That was-- [LAUGHS] IT not mean I like. You see, because it was all time problem. If you do something not right way, you'd be sent to concentration camp, or to labor camp to Germany. It was not so-- It was risky proposition.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you get paid to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, pay was like usual in Poland. This was not the important thing, what you earn. Important was the [NON-ENGLISH], the paper. What save you. You cannot be sent to camp to Germany. That was the problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, while you were working, did you see any atrocities or killings of Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, that was first two years. Not really two years because later, I start to help my father. And this first time, all Jewish must have the band. You know, German-- the army German was not so bad. And the SS and, you know, Gestapo and also the gendarme was feeling the Jewish are not people, not human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if German go there, the Jewish people are supposed to take the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the head-- out and stand straight facing to the German. For me, was humiliated, right? If somebody do the same guy, the same man mean not animal, only men, doing for the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's was first step. Later, did the same with Polish people. [LAUGHS] Only with Polish it was tougher, because from beginning, was each day on the street was name-- how many people be today killed. And German mean SS not-- If I talk to a German, mean I am talking to representative of German nation. SS, Gestapo, and police, these people was animal from nature. Maybe one of thousand was-- has human heart. That's-- we don't know. And each day, you know how many people was killed in Warsaw by prepared terror. And for me, personally, because you mentioned me personally, I can give you some sample. One day, close to my father place, I go to the corner, was snow and stop snowing. I turn the corner and I walk over something like rubber. I will look closer-- is all blood, because some snow covered the blood. And on the wall was-- on the six foot high, you know, the bullet, bullet, and brain-- human brain on the wall and the fragment pieces of the flesh from the shooting. X number of people was shot-- created terror. That was the reason German doing this, because if one German was killed or something like this, was right away handled. Polish people was shoot by German. And that's one reason. Other, I was in the place during the day. German bring maybe 15-- no, 15, maybe 20 people, something like this, and prepare put against the wall, in Warsaw. And the people are not screaming. Not give the voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what happened? Because I was watching maybe 50 meter from the 150, 200 foot. And German like you must see what happens if you not listen us. And what happened? How did German do it is put the gypsum in the mouth the people who be shot. And turn their hands like this, and shoot. And that was, I learn from this moment, the people are doing this way-- not because the people who was shot screaming or holler-- leave-- could leave Poland, or something like this. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And before was done by Polish guys this way. Later, the German put the gypsum in the mouth. And this was public execution and mostly was done by SS. And pick up the people with high education, because these people who was arrested in a special place, later, they were selected-- professor, doctors, Polish or not Polish, Jewish or not Jewish. Evangelical and not Evangelical. That was not important. Important all professor was killed in Krakow, Warsaw, and also Poznan, you see. That mean intelligentsia. That's German doing first to Polish people, generally speaking. Before the war, between the Polish and Jewish was not-- especially intelligent people was not the big difference. The intelligent people, educated, feel, I live here. I eat here. I breathe this air. I am Polish. Only I am Jewish faith. I am Evangelic faith. I am Catholic faith. That was before the war. After the war, was a little bit different story. And that was the personal feeling. And each day, each day if you pass from here, there by train or by-- by foot, you was not [INAUDIBLE] by SS. That was-- thank God. And that-- I keep this job from beginning. Later there, I joined my father's small staff, and, you know-- because from beginning, it was not work. Sweater or something like this, was not work because was not supply, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was also looking for some other jobs because was not work. And after there were was-- start supply a little bit, and my father opened a small business. I joined my father. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And also was [NON-ENGLISH], mean paper, you work. You are a working person, not without work. Who is without work, got to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you join your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] '42, '41, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see any persecution of the Jews in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that was-- you see, I have not the recollection really, the first. Each day from 1940 probably one, after the ghetto was created, each day was-- the German show small children, mostly from to ghetto, shot. That was popular. Mean German know the children from the ghetto-- I'm talking about 1942. '43, '42. There was hunger in the ghetto. The small children go out through the same hole, and bring the food from Aryan side. And if German see this-- gendarme, special gendarme, not popular army person. A German army person mean regular Wehrmacht-- only gendarme-- kill you right away on the spot. That was popular. And later, there-- first, how I can do-- first-- I was each day-- later, after was created ghetto, I not need go to ghetto because my father received this special privilege go to buy some small stuff in ghetto, and I have, too. And we go each day. Me or my father go there and bring something to eat or bring something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was representative, like I said before, the toys manufactured in ghetto. And I bring the money back. That's was not against the law, against the [NON-ENGLISH.] Only was against the [NON-ENGLISH] How do you call? You cannot bring the food to ghetto. Money, all right. No food. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, that was very simple-- German like kill by hunger the people in the ghetto. And especially, generally speaking, ghetto was created in place where was most crowded Jewish people. I mean [NON-ENGLISH] , where was before the war, that was Jewish section. And, generally speaking, Jewish people like be together. That's natural thing because, first, speak the same language. Some Jewish people not speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, Jewish. That's half German, half Jewish. That's not the-- that's some people, old people. And what I am driving to this, this was-- and also from religion point of view. What we expect? These people, Saturday was Sabbath, right? The people has own religion, and was quite-- everybody dressed-- before the war, was dressed nice and stay not working. And that was his privilege, and in Poland, nobody, nobody has against this. You can work. You are Jewish, or Saturday, fine. You have Islam, and you have Friday free day, fine. That was no problem. And I am adding maybe just detail. And the ghetto was created in place where it is most, by square meter, occupied places. German created ghetto push everybody from the other small town there, and created terrible situation. Terrible. Up to do this moment, you know, was not food, was not supply. And on-- and on each place where you cross the ghetto and Aryan part, was one gendarme, one Polish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] policeman, and-- before the war, policeman, and also one Jewish policeman dressed the same like Polish guys. A little bit different.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you go through the border, through the gates. If gendarme see you and you have something that was sticking out-- you know? Or many people from ghetto going to work in-- for German-- in Aryan part. And the people bring sometime food. Hide the food in-- any place. If you was catch like this, you was beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people was beaten very bad. And if you are Christian and going single, for example, and you have something, and gendarme see you, you were arrested and sent to Polish prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick this up on the next tape.
